FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, n=10,755 participants ages 9-10 years from 21 recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with multiple linear regression examining associations between sociodemographic factors and 6 screen time modalities

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in The Journal of Pediatrics

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable estimates found (no time on specific platforms, MPIQ, SMAQ, or other phone/SM measures reported as main variables). Step 2: Composite includes TV, video games, social media, texting, video chat—bundles TV (non-phone/non-SM) with phone/SM items without separate breakout. Other side: all predictors are demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, income, education), not individual-trait-level per §2.4 definition

notes: Excluded at FT-EC2: composite screen time with demographic-only predictors

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, December 2020 COVID-19 rapid response survey, n=4,106 adolescents ages 12-14

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with modified Poisson regression examining associations between COVID-19 information sources and masking behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Preventive Medicine Reports

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social media and television media are measured as information exposure frequency (predictors), not as qualifying phone/SM variables per Step 1 criteria (no time-on-SM, SMAQ, platform-specific items). Step 2: No composite of phone/SM use items formed. Other side: masking behavior is a health behavior outcome, not an individual-trait-level variable per §2.4. The relationship analyzed is information source exposure → protective health behavior, not SM/phone use paired with individual-trait variable

notes: Excluded at FT-EC2: information exposure (not qualifying SM/phone variable) paired with health behavior outcome (not individual-trait variable)

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 follow-up 2018-2020, n=8,753 adolescents ages 10-14 from 21 recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with multiple linear regression examining associations between sociodemographic factors and three problematic screen use measures (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). Per §2.4 binding clarification (1): "Problematic-use scales are SM/phone variables, not 'individual-trait' outcomes." All analyses pair sociodemographic predictors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, income, education, parent marital status)—demographic-only or family-structure (not individual-trait-level)—with VGAQ/SMAQ/MPIQ as dependent variables. Other side is demographic-only, not individual-trait-level per §2.4 definition

notes: Excluded at FT-EC2: problematic-use scales (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ) as DV with demographic-only predictors; per binding rule §2.4(1), scales measure SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes
